article:
Chunk # 21.
Preserve the original color of the reacting bands by
ﬁxing the gel slices in BN gel ﬁxing solution (with gentle
agitation at room temperature for 30 min) ( seeNotes 9
and10).Study of Liver Mitochondrial OXPHOS by (1D) BN-PAGE 414 Determine Complex V activity by incubation of BN-PAGE gels
in Complex V buffer with gentle agitation at room tempera-
ture The band color (white) needs several hours to appear and
be detected Stop the reaction in 50% methanol and wash the
gels with distilled water 5 Scan the gels using an imaging system and express the areas of
the colored bands as absolute values (arbitrary units) 4 Notes
1 When working on subcellular compartments, as in the case of
mitochondria, the purity of the preparation is critical Indeed,
during mitochondrial preparation, several proteins can be
co-isolated (i.e., from endoplasmic reticulum or other organ-
elle membranes in close contact with the mitochondria),
impairing quantitative and qualitative analyses, and thus alter-
ing data interpretation Several methods have been applied in
an effort to obtain pure mitochondria from tissues and cells,
including differential centrifugation; density gradient centrifu-
gation with Percoll ™[36], Nycodenz [ 37], metrizamide [ 38]
or sucrose [ 39] free-ﬂow electrophoresis [ 40],and kit-based
methods [ 41] Mitochondria can also be highly puriﬁed by
immunoisolation (by means of mitochondria-speciﬁc antibo-
dies) although the costs are high and sample retrieval is low
[42] The purity of the mitochondrial preparation—a funda-
mental issue above all others when performing quantitative
analyses of proteins with different subcellular localizations—
can be tested by measuring marker-enzyme activities (such as
citrate synthase) and/or by western blotting for speciﬁc mar-
kers (i.e., voltage-dependent anion channels (VDACs) as posi-
tive control or histones as negative control) 2 Mitochondrial heterogeneity with respect to sedimentation
characteristics, chemical makeup, and enzyme activities has
long been recognized An optional method to isolate mito-
chondria to distinguish “heavy” (1000 /C g), “medium”
(3000 /C g), and “light” (10,000 /C g) mitochondrial subfrac-
tions has been suggested [ 43] The 3000 /C gfraction repre-
sents relatively intact mitochondria and is characterized by high
oxidation rates and good respiratory control Even lighter liver
mitochondria can be isolated at 27,000 /C g However, in this
case, the high gravitational ﬁeld can signiﬁcantly affect the
integrity of the obtained fraction [ 44] To obtain the light fraction, the supernatant from the
centrifugation at 3000 /C ghas to be centrifuged at
10,000 /C gfor 10 min at 4/C14C To obtain the lighter fraction,
the supernatant obtained at 10,000 /C g has to be centrifuged at42 Elena Silvestri et al.27,000 /C gfor 10 min at 4/C14C The pellets obtained in these
two separate steps have to be washed twice by adding the
sucrose buffer again and then resuspending by pipetting Then, the suspension is centrifuged again for 10 min either at
10,000 or at 27,000 /C g Resuspend the mitochondria pellets
in sucrose buffer 3 At this step, the protocol can be temporarily stopped by freez-
ing the mitochondrial preparation before membrane solubili-
zation A freeze-thaw cycle helps to break the membrane and
facilitates respiratory chain complexes solubilization during the
subsequent steps for BN-PAGE 4 The samples can be stored at /C080/C14C for several months before
loading on the gel 5 Commercial ready-to-use blue native gels are also available
(Invitrogen, Native PAGE Novex Bis-Tris Gel system) 6 Gels can be stored at 4/C14C for 1 month if the overlay buffer is
renewed at ~3-day intervals 7 For better band detection, the cathode buffer blue can be
removed after one-third of the gel is run and replaced with
cathode buffer before completing the electrophoresis 8 Destaining is important for reducing the gel background signal
and the signal from nonspeciﬁc staining 9 The time latency for color appearance for tissues other than
liver can be longer In any case, in vivo iodothyronine adminis-
tration or changes in the thyroid state of the whole animal can
inﬂuence time latency for color appearance 10 To save materials and reagents, the gel can be cut to the band of
interest 11 The Coomassie Blue G-250 dye, used in BN-PAGE, interferes
with in-gel ﬂuorescence detection and some in-gel catalytic
activity assays, as in the case of Complex III This problem
can be overcome by performing high-resolution clear native
electrophoresis (CNE) The basic separation principles and the
performance of BN-PAGE and CNE are markedly different
although the experimental setups are largely identical, except
for the addition of Coomassie Blue G-250 dye to the sample
and cathode buffer for BN-PAGE

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:

1. Fix the gel slices in BN gel fixing solution with gentle agitation at room temperature for 30 min.
2. Incubate BN-PAGE gels in Complex V buffer with gentle agitation at room temperature.
3. Allow the band color to appear and be detected, which may take several hours.
4. Stop the reaction by immersing the gels in 50% methanol and wash with distilled water.
5. Scan the gels using an imaging system and express the areas of the colored bands as absolute values.

Notes on Incompleteness or Uncertainties:
- The article does not provide the complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. It only mentions the steps related to gel fixing and scanning.
- The specific method of mitochondrial isolation is not described, and it is mentioned that several methods can be used, such as differential centrifugation and density gradient centrifugation.
- The article suggests using marker-enzyme activities or western blotting to test the purity of the mitochondrial preparation, but does not provide details on how to perform these tests.
- The article mentions an optional method to isolate different mitochondrial subfractions based on sedimentation characteristics, but does not provide the complete protocol for this method.
- The article suggests freezing the mitochondrial preparation before membrane solubilization, but does not provide details on the subsequent steps for BN-PAGE.

Conclusion:
The article provides limited information on the steps for mitochondrial isolation. It mentions fixing the gel slices, incubating the gels, stopping the reaction, and scanning the gels. However, it does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, including details on the specific method used and the subsequent steps for BN-PAGE. Additional information is needed to perform the complete mitochondrial isolation protocol accurately.

summary:
**Iteration 7:**
Preserve the original color of the reacting bands by fixing the gel slices in BN gel fixing solution for 30 min. Study Liver Mitochondrial OXPHOS by (1D) BN-PAGE 414. Determine Complex V activity by incubating BN-PAGE gels in Complex V buffer. Band color (white) appears after several hours. Stop reaction in 50% methanol and wash gels with distilled water. Scan gels using an imaging system and express colored band areas as absolute values. Ensure purity of mitochondrial preparation by measuring marker-enzyme activities (e.g., citrate synthase) and performing western blotting for specific markers (e.g., voltage-dependent anion channels as positive control, histones as negative control). Isolate mitochondria using techniques like differential centrifugation, density gradient centrifugation (e.g., Percoll, Nycodenz, metrizamide, sucrose), free-flow electrophoresis, and immunoisolation. Assess mitochondrial heterogeneity based on sedimentation characteristics, chemical makeup, and enzyme activities. Use blue native gels or the Native PAGE Novex Bis-Tris Gel system for visualization of respiratory chain complexes. Analyze endoplasmic reticulum and organelle membranes quantitatively for proteins. Measure oxidation rates and respiratory control in liver mitochondria. Employ sucrose buffer and freeze-thaw cycle for membrane solubilization during BN-PAGE. Utilize Invitrogen and cathode buffer blue for gel electrophoresis. Remove cathode buffer for better band detection. Perform destaining to reduce background signal from nonspecific staining. Consider time latency for color appearance in different tissues and the influence of iodothyronine administration or changes in thyroid state. Employ clear native electrophoresis and in-gel fluorescence detection for gel analysis, especially for in-gel catalytic activity assays of Complex III. Store gels at 4°C and renew overlay buffer regularly to maintain gel stability. Cut gels to separate bands of interest for further analysis. Be aware of interference from Coomassie Blue G-250 dye in BN-PAGE and consider high-resolution clear native electrophoresis as an alternative.

topics.topic: Preservation of gel color in BN-PAGE
topics.justification: The summary mentions the importance of preserving the original color of the reacting bands in BN gel fixing solution.

topics.topic: Study of Liver Mitochondrial OXPHOS using (1D) BN-PAGE
topics.justification: The summary mentions the specific technique used to study Liver Mitochondrial OXPHOS.

topics.topic: Determination of Complex V activity in BN-PAGE gels
topics.justification: The summary mentions the method of determining Complex V activity by incubating BN-PAGE gels in Complex V buffer.

topics.topic: Detection and measurement of marker-enzyme activities and specific markers for mitochondrial purity assessment
topics.justification: The summary mentions the techniques used to ensure the purity of mitochondrial preparation, including measuring marker-enzyme activities and performing western blotting for specific markers.

topics.topic: Techniques for mitochondrial isolation
topics.justification: The summary mentions various techniques for isolating mitochondria, including differential centrifugation, density gradient centrifugation, free-flow electrophoresis, and immunoisolation.

topics.topic: Assessment of mitochondrial heterogeneity based on sedimentation characteristics, chemical makeup, and enzyme activities
topics.justification: The summary mentions the criteria used to assess mitochondrial heterogeneity.

topics.topic: Visualization of respiratory chain complexes using blue native gels or the Native PAGE Novex Bis-Tris Gel system
topics.justification: The summary mentions the methods used for visualizing respiratory chain complexes.

topics.topic: Quantitative analysis of proteins in endoplasmic reticulum and organelle membranes
topics.justification: The summary mentions the quantitative analysis of proteins in endoplasmic reticulum and organelle membranes.

topics.topic: Measurement of oxidation rates and respiratory control in liver mitochondria
topics.justification: The summary mentions the measurement of oxidation rates and respiratory control in liver mitochondria.

topics.topic: Solubilization of membranes during BN-PAGE using sucrose buffer and freeze-thaw cycle
topics.justification: The summary mentions the technique used for membrane solubilization during BN-PAGE.

topics.topic: Gel electrophoresis using Invitrogen and cathode buffer blue
topics.justification: The summary mentions the specific products used for gel electrophoresis.

topics.topic: Destaining to reduce background signal in gel staining
topics.justification: The summary mentions the technique of destaining to reduce background signal from nonspecific staining.

topics.topic: Consideration of time latency for color appearance in different tissues and the influence of iodothyronine administration or changes in thyroid state
topics.justification: The summary mentions the factors that can influence the time latency for color appearance in different tissues.

topics.topic: Clear native electrophoresis and in-gel fluorescence detection for gel analysis, especially for in-gel catalytic activity assays of Complex III
topics.justification: The summary mentions the use of clear native electrophoresis and in-gel fluorescence detection for gel analysis.

topics.topic: Storage and maintenance of gel stability
topics.justification: The summary mentions the storage and maintenance requirements for gels to maintain gel stability.

topics.topic: Separation of bands of interest for further analysis
topics.justification: The summary mentions the need to cut gels to separate bands of interest for further analysis.

topics.topic: Interference from Coomassie Blue G-250 dye in BN-PAGE and alternative high-resolution clear native electrophoresis
topics.justification: The summary mentions the interference caused by Coomassie Blue G-250 dye in BN-PAGE and suggests an alternative method.

hypothetical_questions.question: How can the original color of the reacting bands be preserved in gel slices during BN-PAGE?
What techniques can be used to isolate mitochondria for the study of liver mitochondrial OXPHOS?
Imagine you are analyzing endoplasmic reticulum and organelle membranes quantitatively for proteins. How would you go about it?
How can the oxidation rates and respiratory control in liver mitochondria be measured?
Have you ever encountered interference from Coomassie Blue G-250 dye in BN-PAGE? If so, how did you address it?
